lesson.title: Antiderivatives & Indefinite Integrals
lesson.body: An antiderivative of f(x) is a function F(x) such that F'(x) = f(x). The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration. Key rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1)+C for n ≠ −1, ∫eˣdx = eˣ+C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x|+C, ∫sin x dx = −cos x+C, ∫cos x dx = sin x+C.

rooms.question: What is ∫(3x² − 2x + 5) dx?
rooms.explanation: Integrate term by term: 3·x³/3 − 2·x²/2 + 5x = x³ − x² + 5x + C.

rooms.question: What is ∫eˣ dx?
rooms.explanation: The exponential function is its own derivative and antiderivative: d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ, so ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C.

rooms.question: Match integrals to their antiderivatives (order from simplest to most complex):
rooms.explanation: Constant → power rule → general power rule → logarithm (the n=−1 case that the power rule can't handle).

rooms.question: If F'(x) = f(x) and G'(x) = f(x), then F(x) − G(x) is:
rooms.explanation: d/dx[F−G] = F' − G' = f − f = 0, so F − G = C. Any two antiderivatives of f differ by a constant — hence +C.

boss.name: The Antiderivative
boss.taunt: Every derivative has a source. Find it, or get lost in the constant.

questions.question: ∫sin(3x) dx = ?
questions.explanation: ∫sin(ax)dx = −cos(ax)/a + C. The chain rule in reverse divides by the inner derivative a=3.

questions.question: A particle has velocity v(t) = 3t² − 2t. If its position at t=0 is s(0) = 4, find s(t).
questions.latex: s(t) = \int v(t)\, dt = t^3 - t^2 + C
questions.explanation: s(t) = ∫(3t²−2t)dt = t³−t²+C. Apply s(0)=4: 0−0+C=4 → C=4, so s(t) = t³−t²+4.

questions.question: ∫(1/(x² + 1)) dx = ?
questions.explanation: This is a standard integral: d/dx[arctan(x)] = 1/(1+x²), so ∫dx/(1+x²) = arctan(x) + C.
